story:
John Waite was a fisherman all his life. A stout man with an untamed beard and a face cut by years of labour, he rises at four, hauls his aching limbs from the rickety bed, and forces his blackened feet into socks and stubborn boots. In the cobwebbed mirror, his bright, fiery eyes burn in a face of leathery lines. He kindles a small fire, boils tea, and takes it with bread and margarine. Heat pushes back the chill that slips through his shirt and the old jumper Ethel knitted before she died. He rinses his cup, pockets his scran tin, pulls on his threadbare coat, and steps into the dark.

His path along the cliff takes two hours, a charcoal world where the only sure guide is the sea to his left, its waves visiting the beach. He knows every rise and dip; his heavy boots rake the grass. Birds wake and sing. Behind him, jagged headlands jut like rock monsters, eroded into shapes like small islands. At last the sun dries his clothes and eases his bones, and thirst draws him toward the little fishing village.

The track meanders down. Whitewash gleams, boats bob at their tethers, walkers mill on the quay. He slips into his old inn, low-beamed and close, the fire roaring, logs cracking. He takes his usual stool, lifts his black tankard, and drinks. Walkers drift in, debating ales, and no one seems to notice him.

Rested, he climbs again, more from habit than strain, the old effort echoing through him. He follows the grey spine of the path until the land drops, then picks his way down the rough trail, hauling himself over slick boulders before stepping the last few feet onto wet brown sand. No print remains where his boots fall; the foam passes through them as if the shore cannot keep his mark.

He sits on a solitary rock, opens the tin, and goes through the motions of eating, remembering the taste of bread, while he studies what remains of his boat. Seaweed and sand half-bury the dead wood. Once it was his pride, the sturdy craft his father handed down, built to carry ten wicker baskets for the catch. Fourteen hours at sea would be enough to feed Ethel and their two boys, with a little left to sell. Out beyond Kernow he learned the moods of water, imagined whales gliding beneath the skin of the sea and mermaids flashing silver in the swell. Memories rise one after another: his wife and small boys, his fellow fishermen, the first time he went out with his father. The sea gods are always stirring.

Then came the storm. One day, he did not return. The ocean took the boat; days later the wreckage washed ashore. John Waite’s body was never found. Only his lost soul keeps the path: out of the ruined cottage at four, along the cliffs through the dark, to the inn where no one notices him, down to the beach and the rotting shrine. In Kernow, if you look long enough, you can still see him walking.
